speaker: interviewer
text: Dave, why don't you introduce us to your family here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Glad to. This is my wife, Helene, of what, 33 years, roughly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost 34. In two weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Jason, number one grandson. Hopefully many more to come. And his mother, my daughter Beverly, beautiful Beverly. And back here, the guy with a straight face is Steven, my older son, and Danny, my younger son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we can go around the room. Just tell me how does it-- you know, obviously, you've been married for a long time, but how has Dave's experiences, you know, what he went through, how did it affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, it's very hard to understand, because I was born just as the war broke out, so I have no real connection to what really happened. I just feel bad for him because he has no parents, and it was a really rough time for him. He went through an awful lot as a child, you know, emotionally and everything else has been rough on him over the years. But thank God he's got his own family now, and he's got healthy children, and a beautiful grandson. And hopefully we can make his life worthwhile for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bev, how, you know, how do-- how do you feel when you think about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like my mom said, it's hard for us to comprehend what he went through, because we weren't there and we've had a good life here. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal life. But it's important that we do know what he went through and make sure that we understand it, and pass it on to our kids so it won't be forgotten, and make sure it doesn't happen again, to anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think about, you know, what his parents had to do, could imagine?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Not at all. Can't imagine. Had to be the hardest thing they've ever had to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dan or Steve? Steve?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess being the oldest son, and the oldest one in the family, I think it was kind of hard for my dad to raise us, because I think he hasn't had parents. He didn't grow up with parents, so me growing up with my father, he didn't have the experience of knowing what it's like to be a son, I think, with a parent, so I think it was rougher. I think there was a lot of experimentation going on with me growing up. [LAUGHTER] He did better on the second take, and so-- I would think that's the main-- main thing there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I think it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on how to relate with us, I think, was probably difficult, not having parents. And at times, maybe we seem spoiled, rather than-- which was probably more just normal. Again, I just think it was-- it probably made it more difficult. At times, it was much easier to relate to the mother growing up than the father. But-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and just knowing about his past-- in fact, we were talking last night, I think there's not a whole lot I even know about him growing up.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was my next question. At what point did you tell your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one of the things that I think I will benefit from this tape is they can, at their own leisure, find out things that I never had time or never had the appropriate situation where I felt they would listen. I understand, they're growing up in an environment where they want to distance themselves from a lot of things that I went through. So it was hard for me to tell them about a lot of things. In fact, I compared a little bit, something I didn't talk to you about, is I told you I grew up in this home, that we were really deprived. But I didn't want anybody to know about it. It was like an embarrassment.

speaker: interviewer
text: In fact, one of the most embarrassing days of my life was my bar mitzvah. What happened was, I belonged to [NON-ENGLISH], which is a Jewish organization, youth organization, Zionist organization, and we used to have meetings once a week, and the rabbi who was a supervisor of the home I was in, said, why don't you invite the group? And I said, Jesus. No way, no how, do I want them here to see the way I live here. I'm totally embarrassed. So I didn't say anything. But guess what? One of the other guys from a different group-- older group, but also from the hostel, came and he invited everybody in my name. So they showed up, and I was so embarrassed. I can't tell you. So, I feel that maybe in some ways, being different is an embarrassment, so I understand the kids felt that way. They weren't like-- they're in school, where everybody is-- peer pressure and everything else. They don't want to say they got a greenhorn or-- that's a dad that's-- you know-- nobody wants to know about it. So I just-- I really didn't push this information on them, because I-- because that's the way I thought they would react. When did you finally find out or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for me that's not how I felt, and I don't think my brothers do either. No. I think that he felt that way, but we-- I know that that's not how we ever felt.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's the reason why I didn't really, you know, talk about it a lot, because I know that's the way I felt. I felt that my problems, I don't want everybody to know about it. It's an embarrassment.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't feel that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, we liked learning-- I just know from-- I can remember, for my whole life knowing about the whole thing. I think we knew about it, probably when we were born, we were probably told kind of, somehow. I mean, I don't remember ever being told. I just knew it all my life. And I think, it's never been an embarrassment to me at all, so I think that's all my dad's perception of it, because I always told my friends my history of my family, and I always tell them what happened to my grandparents and stuff like that. And in fact, in college, I studied a lot of that, and actually I got into it and wanted to learn more about what happened in the history of my family.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fact that one set of grandparents, you never met.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well that's what I was going to say. How did you explain? How did you feel about not having grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we never had them, so it wasn't abnormal for us because we never had them. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that's interesting. So you studied about it, so you really do--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've thought about it, but I think this tape, at least, I feel like I'm getting it off my chest without boring them, or having them sit through it. I mean, it's there. If they're interested, fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you guys want to say to your dad or add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just that I love you and I'm sorry that you've felt this way all these years. Because it's not true. We've always wanted to know, and we've always cared and feel that you had a hard time.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact I'm looking forward to watching the tape. I want to see what he has to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I mean, I felt what I've known is about how he grew up was just the total basics. No details, just he got sent to England. At 17, he went to Israel, then he came to New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or 16. And I don't even have those details right, obviously. So, yeah. I think we're all looking forward to learning.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's an amazing man and it's an amazing story. Thank you so much for being here. Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whoops. What do you have to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you have to say? What do you think about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you have to say to grandpa?

speaker: interviewer
text: What are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a travel document that we were given on entering England. It's-- I think it's stamped on the back. It says the right to land in England or whatever, and it's probably the only picture I have of my childhood, believe it or not. And it's-- it says, Pinkasovic, Isidor, which is the name that I used when I-- which was my name when I came to England. It says male sex, sex, male, I mean. And date of birth, which I don't agree with. Says April 3rd-- my birthday I use is April 12th. And it says place of birth Negrovic, which-- I don't have any proof of it, but that's what they said on the document.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakia. I don't have any birth certificate. And then it has my parents' name, which I'm not sure is accurate either, because I knew them as Mordechai and Esther, but this could be their non-Jewish names. Or-- And then down below, it says fluchtlingslager, which I understand means refugee camp, Bratislava. Which is, I believe, Slovakia, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was right before we-- we-- I guess docked in-- before we landed in England. So-- being an English document, it probably was made when we landed. Those pictures were probably attached to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because there's a date on the back that says April 1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when we landed, yeah. It's a British document, I'm pretty sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: The British issued this to you when you landed.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, I'm not sure exactly when this was issued, because I've seen documents identical to that with a German swastika stamped on it-- the ones that left Czechoslovakia closer to the beginning of the war. So the Germans stamped it, so in order to stamp it, the document had to be issued before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was maybe issued to you in Czechoslovakia before you went to England.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or in England, sent-- given to me in Czechoslovakia to be approved by the authorities before we left, possibly. I don't know. Because I-- yeah, definitely, of that magazine there, you will-- you will see the swastika.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, and what are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture from the Jewish Boys' Home in Ely. I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Ely, in which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: England. Ely in North England, and that's where we were evacuated, and that's the Boys' Home with Mr. Kahn, Rabbi Kahn, excuse me. The one with the beard, near the right side. I'm on the bottom row, third from the left, on the left, the third. That's me. I guess then I was Isidor Pinkasovic, today Dave Lux. And this picture was taken, I believe, in 1942 or 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Ely from 1939 until about a year after the war, which is 1946. And-- And this is a close-up of the picture we just saw. I'm on the left, Dave Lux, and on the right of me is my brother, my older brother who still keeps the name of Pinkasovic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And his first name, well, was Herman in England, but in Israel, he uses Yaakov Svi, which is his Jewish name, Pinkasovic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many boys were in the Ely Jewish Home?

speaker: interviewee
text: It--it varied. There were times I think we were as many as 50 or 60, but some were temporary, I guess, and some, once they were working age, they moved out and got jobs and for various reasons, but at this time we probably were like 30. This is a picture taken in 1951. I was, I think, in the middle of my basic training. I just got into the army. That uniform you see is brand new. And on the left, of course, that's me, Dave Lux, and on the right is my brother. I must have had a day off of something, had a pass. That's my brother, Jacob Pinkasovic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at that time was still--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sorry. At that time my name was Israel. Israel Pinkasovic. I didn't change my name until I came-- until after I was married, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken? Where in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. It looks like it was done at a photo studio, though. It doesn't look like a snapshot. I'm pretty sure it was done at a photo studio.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which city, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would guess it was Tel Aviv, which was the most convenient location for us to get something done like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in boot camp at this point in the Israeli army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Apparently one of the reasons that I went to get this picture taken, I probably needed some pictures for some other documents, so we shot this one also with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here? Let's say. I'm not even 18 yet, because I was 18 in April and this was January or February. Same year.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, where is this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taken in Tel Aviv, close to the ocean. I'm sorry, Mediterranean Sea. And I guess on the left is my friend Nathan Baruch. He was just about my best friend, I guess, and on the right that's me-- at the time, Israel Pinkasovic. And we had a 24-hour pass, probably. We didn't frequently get anything longer than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken-- this being black and white, so you can't tell. We're both wearing red berets. And I can see by my friend, Nathan, has two stripes, so that means we've been in there for a while, because he got those stripes probably after we'd been in the-- in the Airborne at least about a year. So I would guess this is 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were-- at what level did you reach in the Airborne?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't get any rank. I was kind of non-cooperative. [LAUGHS] He was more of a-- a career. You know, he signed on for longer. I just wanted to get it done and get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost two and a half years. And then afterwards, of course, seven years more of reserves. If I would have stayed in Israel, I might still be there. [LAUGHS] They keep you in the reserves forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is your wedding photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep, that's what it is. That's Helene, used to be Rudolph, on the left. And at the time I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Isidor Pinkasovic. I guess, on the right. That's-- that's before I changed my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1962. See, I remember my wedding day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- wait a minute-- that was taken, yeah, at the wedding. I don't think we took pictures before the wedding. So that was in Cleveland on March 31, 1962.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you change your name to Dave Lux?

speaker: interviewee
text: This happened when we came to California and I had to get licenses because I went into real estate and stuff, so I thought it was a good time to get my name thing straightened out that I wanted to do. I just didn't get a chance. I wanted to do that before I get real estate license, then I-- I wouldn't have to change everything again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same--we came to California right after the wedding. Actually, we came here for our honeymoon, I think for about two weeks or so, just to check it out, I guess. And we liked what we saw. We just flew back, packed our bags, drove out. That was it. This picture was taken at my brother's home in Jerusalem, and starting from the left, that's Coby, that's my brother's son-in-law, married to his daughter Eti. He's holding Asaf, his first child. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: The woman in the red?

speaker: interviewee
text: The woman in the red, in the center, is Mali. That's my brother's daughter-in-law, and she is holding a newborn, I guess, or pretty young child, Dov. And behind her, over above ahead, is my brother Jacob Pinkasovic. And next to him, on the right of him, is Shula Pinkasovic, his wife. Next to her, right, going towards the right, is Eti his daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sitting on the ground or on the couch?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- no, top right. Top right. Sitting next to Shula.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm sorry, her name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eti, or Esther. Eti for short. And sitting next Eti is Helene, my wife-- Helene Lux. And then, going in a circle, to the lower beneath Shula and Eti in the middle, is Ricky. That's my brothers younger daughter, and under that is Tal, which is-- let's see, that would be Mali's son, the one in the red dress, and Motke's son, who is not in the picture. He's probably taking it. This picture was taken at the same time, 1989, the same place at my brother's house. Starting from the left, that's my brother again, Jacob Pinkasovic, and top center is Avi, his youngest son, and beneath him is Ricky again, his sister, my brother's younger daughter. And on the right is my wife, Helene, again-- Helene Lux.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in Jerusalem?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jerusalem, 1989. This picture was probably taken at the same time, 1989, at my brother's house. And starting from the left, that's Mali, my son's-- my-- his oldest son's wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother's.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's, I'm sorry. My brother's oldest son's wife, Mali. Next to her, going to the right, is Helene, my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helene Lux?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helene Lux, and she's holding, oh, let's see, that'll be Dov, the youngest son they have. And next-- and next to him, going to the right, that's me, Dave Lux. Next to me is Tal. That's Mali and Motke. By the way, the name is Pinkasovic, like my brother. That's their oldest son, Tal, and on the far right, that's Motke. That's my brother's oldest son. The reason that we don't have too many pictures of him, because he's the one that took the pictures, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's named after your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's named after my father, Mordechai, or Motke for short, Pinkasovic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was also taken in Jerusalem?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jerusalem, 1989, at my brother's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dave, what is this that we're looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a souvenir magazine, a brochure, it says from the Kindertransport Reunion in 1989. And you notice on the right bottom corner, there's a travel document, the photograph, just like the one I have. The only difference, it has a German swastika stamped on it, which indicates this was much closer to the beginning of the war when the Germans had more control over who was coming and going. Probably what happened is it cost more money to get them out at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this reunion at?

speaker: interviewee
text: The reunion was in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is where you met Mr. Winton?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's when I first found out, you know, the truth about how I got there. Which-- yeah, it took 50 years for me to find that out. But yeah, that was kind of a-- looking at those documents with a swastika on it, it kind of-- it kind of brought a chill to my spine when I saw those.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some people got out much closer to the war. Like I left in April, I believe. I think I arrived in April, and these were closer, like July. So they barely-- they barely made it. And how many people attended this reunion?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were several thousand. At first I said I think 10,000, and that's not true. I think there were 10,000 total of Kindertransport. I think there was either two or something like that. 2,000 or 3,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were able to meet Mr. Winton?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from what I understand, he was responsible for bringing out about a few hundred people-- 500, or something like that. And that's pretty good for an individual.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a great effort. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1987 at our 25th anniversary. This is a picture of the fruits of our marriage, I guess, including starting from the left, Danny, Daniel Lux. In the middle is Beverly Lux, and on the right is the oldest, Steven Lux.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what can I say? It was our anniversary, 25th anniversary, and that's a picture that I keep with pride. Thank you very much.
